content:
# Leviticus Chapter 4 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 59r–59v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_004_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_004_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** The LORD spoke to Moses: **2** "Speak to the Israelites and say: When a person sins unintentionally — by doing something the LORD has commanded must not be done — the following applies.

**3** If it is the anointed priest who has sinned, and his sin brings guilt on the people, he shall bring to the LORD, as his purification offering for the sin he has committed, an unblemished young bull. **4** He shall bring the bull to the entrance of the tent of meeting before the LORD. He shall lay his hand on the head of the bull and slaughter it before the LORD. **5** The anointed priest shall then take some of the bull's blood and carry it into the tent of meeting. **6** The priest shall dip his finger in the blood and sprinkle some of it seven times before the LORD, in front of the veil of the sanctuary. **7** The priest shall also put some of the blood on the horns of the altar of fragrant incense before the LORD — the altar that stands inside the tent of meeting. Then he shall pour out all the remaining blood at the base of the burnt-offering altar that stands at the entrance to the tent of meeting. **8** He shall remove all the fat of the bull that is used for the purification offering: the fat that covers the inner organs, all the fat that clings to them, **9** both kidneys with the fat on them near the loins, and the lobe of the liver, which he shall remove with the kidneys — **10** just as it is removed from the ox of the fellowship sacrifice. The priest shall burn them on the burnt-offering altar. **11** But the bull's hide and all its flesh, along with its head, its legs, its inner organs and its dung — **12** the whole bull — he shall carry outside the camp to a ceremonially clean place, to the ash heap, and burn it on a wood fire. It shall be burned there on the ash heap.

**13** If the whole community of Israel sins unintentionally and the matter escapes the notice of the assembly, so that they do something forbidden by any of the LORD's commands and bear guilt — **14** then, when the sin they have committed becomes known, the assembly shall bring a young bull as a purification offering and present it before the tent of meeting. **15** The elders of the community shall lay their hands on the head of the bull before the LORD, and the bull shall be slaughtered before the LORD. **16** The anointed priest shall carry some of the bull's blood into the tent of meeting. **17** He shall dip his finger in the blood and sprinkle it seven times before the LORD, in front of the veil. **18** He shall put some of the blood on the horns of the altar that stands before the LORD inside the tent of meeting, then pour out all the remaining blood at the base of the burnt-offering altar at the entrance of the tent of meeting. **19** He shall remove all its fat and burn it on the altar. **20** He shall do with this bull exactly as he did with the bull for the priest's own purification offering. In this way the priest will make atonement for them, and they will be forgiven. **21** He shall carry the bull outside the camp and burn it just as the first bull was burned. This is the purification offering for the assembly.

**22** When a leader sins unintentionally and does something forbidden by any of the commands of the LORD his God — **23** when he is made aware of his sin, he shall bring as his offering a male goat without defect. **24** He shall lay his hand on the head of the goat and slaughter it in the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered before the LORD; it is a purification offering. **25** The priest shall take some of the blood of the purification offering on his finger and put it on the horns of the burnt-offering altar, then pour out the remaining blood at the base of the altar. **26** He shall burn all its fat on the altar, just as the fat of the fellowship sacrifice is burned. In this way the priest will make atonement for the leader's sin, and he will be forgiven.

**27** If any member of the community sins unintentionally by doing something forbidden by any of the LORD's commands and becomes guilty — **28** when the sin they have committed becomes known to them, they shall bring as their offering a female goat without defect, as the purification offering for the sin they committed. **29** They shall lay their hand on the head of the purification offering and slaughter it at the place of the burnt offering. **30** The priest shall take some of its blood on his finger and put it on the horns of the burnt-offering altar, then pour out all the remaining blood at the base of the altar. **31** He shall remove all its fat, just as the fat is removed from the fellowship sacrifice, and burn it on the altar as a fragrant aroma to the LORD. In this way the priest will make atonement for them, and they will be forgiven.

**32** If they bring a lamb as their purification offering, they shall bring a female without defect. **33** They shall lay their hand on the head of the purification offering and slaughter it at the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered. **34** The priest shall take some of the blood of the purification offering on his finger and put it on the horns of the burnt-offering altar, then pour out all the remaining blood at the base of the altar. **35** He shall remove all its fat, just as the fat of the lamb is removed from the fellowship sacrifice, and the priest shall burn it on the altar on top of the fire-offerings to the LORD. In this way the priest will make atonement for the sin they have committed, and they will be forgiven.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Chattat — sin as missing the mark.** The Hebrew word translated "purification offering" throughout this chapter is *chattat*. English Bibles traditionally render it "sin offering," but the word does double duty in Hebrew: *chattat* means both the sin itself and the offering that deals with it, and many scholars now prefer "purification offering" because the ritual primarily purifies the sanctuary that sin has contaminated rather than simply punishing the sinner. PNEUMA uses "purification offering" throughout for this reason. But the underlying verb *chata* carries an image worth holding: it means to miss the mark, to veer off the path, to fail to reach the target. Sin in this chapter is not rebellion, not defiance, not the raised fist. It is the archer's arrow that falls short or flies wide. The whole architecture of Leviticus 4 is built for that kind of failure — the inadvertent stumble, the overlooked boundary, the misstep taken in ignorance. Deliberate, high-handed sin (*yad ramah*, "with a high hand") has no remedy here. This chapter is pastoral care for the human tendency to fall short without quite meaning to.

**Bi-shgagah — the theology of the unintentional.** The phrase that opens the whole chapter — *asher tach'ta bi-shgagah*, "who sins unintentionally" — deserves more attention than it usually receives. *Shgagah* comes from a root meaning to wander, to stray, to go astray by mistake. This is not moral carelessness; it is the structural limitation of human knowledge. You cannot keep every boundary you do not know is there. Yet the text insists: even unwitting violation of God's commands creates a real problem that requires a real remedy. The guilt is actual, not merely nominal. The offering is real blood, real death, real cost. Leviticus 4 refuses to trivialize unintentional sin by calling it not-really-sin, while simultaneously refusing to treat it as the same gravity as deliberate rebellion. The chapter opens a category of moral failure the modern world tends to flatten: the genuine wrong done without malicious intent, which still creates a wound that must be healed.

**Kohen ha-mashiach — the anointed priest.** Verse 3 introduces a title that will echo across the entire Hebrew Bible: *ha-kohen ha-mashiach* — the anointed priest. *Mashiach* simply means "anointed one" — one who has been set apart by the pouring of oil. This is its earliest appearance in Leviticus and one of the earliest priestly uses in the Torah. The same word will later describe kings, prophets, and ultimately become the title (Messiah) for Israel's eschatological deliverer. That the word begins here — in the sacrificial instructions, describing the high priest who bears Israel's sin before God — is theologically suggestive. The anointed one is not first a warrior-king but a mediator who enters the sacred space carrying blood. Every subsequent use of *mashiach* in the Hebrew tradition carries this priestly shadow: the anointed one stands between the holy God and the failing people, bringing something to restore what sin has broken.

**The priest's sin burdens the people (v. 3).** The most striking theological claim in the opening case is almost offhand: the anointed priest's sin, if it occurs, "brings guilt on the people." The word is *le-asham ha-am* — to make the people guilty, or to bring guilt down upon them. The high priest does not sin in isolation. His moral state is structurally connected to the moral state of the community. He stands in the breach; if he falls, the breach widens for everyone. This is a profound ancient understanding of representative moral weight — the person who enters the sanctuary on behalf of others cannot simply sin privately. Leadership in Leviticus is not a matter of power; it is a matter of moral burden. The higher the role, the more elaborate the purification required, and the more the community depends on the leader's ritual integrity.

**Blood sprinkled before the veil — nazah, not zaraq.** This is the most theologically loaded technical distinction in the chapter. For the priest's sin (vv. 6–7) and the congregation's sin (vv. 17–18), the blood is *sprinkled* (*hizzah* from *nazah*) seven times before the inner veil — the curtain that separates the outer holy place from the Most Holy Place. For the leader (v. 25) and the common person (v. 30), blood is applied only to the horns of the outer altar; there is no sprinkling, and no movement toward the inner veil. The verb *zaraq* (dashing or splashing, as in the fellowship offering) is not used here. The *nazah* sprinkling is precise, delicate — the kind of application that makes its way into the most sacred space. The closer to the innermost sanctuary the blood must go, the higher the rank of the person whose sin is being addressed. This is not merely procedural variation; it is a map of how seriously different kinds of representative failure threaten the dwelling of God. When the whole assembly sins, the sanctuary itself is contaminated at the deepest level, and the blood must penetrate deeper to cleanse it.

**The fourfold hierarchy — rank, blood, and access.** The chapter's structure is elegant and intentional. Four cases, descending in social rank: the anointed priest (vv. 3–12), the whole congregation (vv. 13–21), a tribal leader or prince (vv. 22–26), a common person (vv. 27–35). Each case carries the same structure — sin, awareness, offering, atonement, forgiveness — but the animals, the blood rituals, and the degree of sanctuary penetration all vary by rank. The priest and the congregation both require a bull and blood taken inside the tent; the leader requires a male goat with blood on the outer altar's horns; the common person requires a female goat or lamb with blood at the same outer altar. The chapter is saying something architectural: the degree of remediation required is proportional to the degree of representative weight carried. A private person's sin is a private wound; the high priest's sin is a national emergency. Everyone has access to atonement, but access is calibrated to responsibility.

**"He will be forgiven" — the declarative.** Four times in this chapter — once for the congregation (v. 20), once for the leader (v. 26), and twice for common persons (vv. 31, 35) — the chapter closes the case with the same declarative sentence: *venislach lo* — "and he will be forgiven," or more literally, "and it will be released for him." *Salach* is the standard Hebrew verb for divine forgiveness, and it appears only ever with God as the subject. Human beings *forgive* one another using a different word (*kaphar* carries the sense of covering or wiping away); God *salachs* — God releases, cancels, declares the matter dissolved. The mechanical completion of the ritual alone does not produce forgiveness; the text expects God to respond to the ritual with an act of divine will. The priest makes atonement (*kaphar*); God forgives (*salach*). PNEUMA renders this "they will be forgiven" — the passive construction deliberately leaving the implied subject as the LORD — because the forgiveness comes from above, not from within the ritual itself.

**The ash heap outside the camp — holiness and pollution together.** The disposal of the priest's bull (vv. 11–12) introduces a striking detail: the whole animal — hide, flesh, head, legs, dung — is carried "outside the camp to a ceremonially clean place, to the ash heap." The Hebrew is *el-maqom tahor*, literally "to a clean place." The ash heap outside the camp is, paradoxically, described as *tahor* — clean, ceremonially pure. The burned remains of the sin offering are not garbage; they are sacred refuse that cannot simply be dumped anywhere. The flesh that has absorbed the sin-contamination must be disposed of at a place that is itself ritually designated for such contact with the holy. The outside-the-camp location matters: whatever has absorbed Israel's impurity must leave the camp to preserve the camp's holiness. But the place where it is burned is *clean* — this is not pollution being discarded but sacred material being removed with dignity. PNEUMA renders "ash heap" to preserve the concrete specificity of the location and its ongoing function.

**LXX (Vaticanus) observations.** Vaticanus pages 92–93 (images pa_0088–0089) preserve Leviticus 4 in characteristic uncial script. Several LXX choices illuminate the Hebrew. At v. 2, the LXX renders *bi-shgagah* as *ἀκουσίως* (*akousios* — "involuntarily, unwillingly"), a precise Greek equivalent that carries the sense of acting against one's will or without volition — a slightly more psychological rendering than the Hebrew "by wandering." The LXX frames the sin as a failure of the will rather than simply a matter of ignorance, which is a subtle but meaningful interpretive shift. At vv. 3 and 5, the LXX consistently translates *kohen ha-mashiach* as *ὁ ἱερεὺς ὁ χριστός* (*ho hiereus ho christos* — "the anointed priest"), preserving the full weight of the title and — notably — using *christos*, the Greek word that will become the theological center of the New Testament. This is the same Vaticanus manuscript from which the Greek New Testament tradition flows; here, in the sacrificial instructions of Leviticus, the word *christos* describes a priest who brings a bull's blood into the sanctuary to atone for sin. The conceptual bridge this creates to Hebrews 9–10 is not accidental. At v. 12, the ash heap location is rendered *εἰς τόπον καθαρόν* (*eis topon katharon* — "into a clean place"), precisely matching *el-maqom tahor* — Vaticanus follows the Hebrew closely here with no interpretive expansion. At vv. 20 and 26, the Greek *ἀφεθήσεται αὐτῷ* (*afethēsetai auto* — "it will be released/forgiven for him") renders *venislach lo* with the verb *aphiemi*, which the New Testament will use consistently for divine forgiveness ("forgive us our debts, as we forgive our debtors"). The Vaticanus *chattat* equivalent is consistently *περὶ τῆς ἁμαρτίας* (*peri tēs hamartias* — "concerning sin" or "for sin"), a phrase Paul will use in Romans 8:3 when he says God sent his Son *peri hamartias* — "as a sin offering." The entire theological vocabulary of atonement in the New Testament has its roots in these Vaticanus pages.

*— PNEUMA*
